title: Replacing a tap washer
subtitle: Isolate the supply, unscrew the headgear, fit a new washer
excerpt: How to stop a dripping tap by replacing the rubber washer in a pillar or bib tap: isolating the water supply, removing the tap headgear, fitting a new washer and O-ring, and reassembling without leaks.
sourceNotes: Standard UK plumbing practice. Collins Complete DIY Manual plumbing chapter. Tap washer types and dimensions from BS 1010 (draw-off taps and stopvalves). Isolation procedure from CIPHE (Chartered Institute of Plumbing and Heating Engineering) guidance.

recipeTools.notes: For loosening a headgear nut that is too tight for the adjustable spanner.

aliases: how to fix a dripping tap

glossaryTerms.definition: A small in-line valve fitted to a pipe run that allows the supply to be shut off at the appliance without shutting off the whole house. Slot-screw or lever-operated.
The main household water shut-off valve, usually under the kitchen sink. Closes the cold water supply to the whole house.

content.text: A tap that drips at the spout when closed is almost always a worn washer. A tap that leaks around the headgear spindle when open is a worn O-ring or packing. Both are fixed the same way: isolate the water, dismantle the tap, replace the rubber, reassemble. The job takes about an hour if you have the right washer size.
on the supply pipe under the tap by a quarter turn so the slot is across the pipe. If there is no isolating valve, close the main household
under the kitchen sink. Open the tap fully to drain the supply pipe.
Prise off the decorative cover on the tap top to expose the central screw (usually a slot-head). Unscrew and remove the cover. This reveals the headgear nut below.

Hold the tap body firmly with one hand or with water-pump pliers around the tap base to prevent turning the whole fitting. Use the adjustable spanner to unscrew the headgear nut anticlockwise (as viewed from above). The headgear assembly lifts out to reveal the washer at the bottom of the spindle.

The washer is held on the jumper (the bottom of the headgear spindle) either by a small nut, or it clips on, or it is just held by a central post. Unscrew the washer retaining nut with small pliers or a screwdriver. Remove the old washer.

Take the old washer to a plumber's merchant or hardware shop and match it by size. Standard sizes are 3/4 inch (19 mm) for bath taps, 1/2 inch (12.7 mm) for basin and kitchen taps. Fit the new washer with the flat face toward the tap seat. Replace any O-rings on the spindle at the same time.

Refit the headgear assembly, screwing the headgear nut clockwise (hand-tight, then a quarter turn with the spanner; do not overtighten). Replace the cover and the top screw. Slowly restore the water supply by opening the isolating valve. Close the tap and check for drips at the spout and at the headgear. A small drip from the headgear means the headgear nut needs one more quarter turn.
Tap still drips after a new washer.

The tap seat inside the tap body is scored or pitted. A worn seat cannot be sealed by a new washer. A tap-seat reseating tool cuts a new flat face inside the tap body; alternatively, a washer-and-seat combination washer can seal a slightly damaged seat.

Apply penetrating oil around the joint and allow 15 minutes to soak. Use the water-pump pliers on the headgear and the adjustable spanner on the nut with opposing force. Do not use excessive force on a ceramic-disc tap headgear; the ceramic discs are brittle and easily cracked.
